suggestion.title: Malicious npm Package Installation with Credential File Access
suggestion.rationale: Article describes worm that 'steals npm, GitHub, AWS, and Kubernetes Secrets From Developers' — requires detection of npm/Node.js process accessing ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.kube, .npmrc after package installation. Pipeline missed entirely.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Alert on node.exe or npm.exe child processes reading credential stores (~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.kube, .npmrc, .env) within 60 seconds of package installation. Flag any process spawned from postinstall/preinstall scripts accessing these paths.

suggestion.title: Package.json Post-Install Script Network Exfiltration
suggestion.rationale: Article implies malware delivered via npm postinstall/preinstall scripts ('stealing' credentials suggests data exfiltration). Pipeline should surface execution + C2 callback pattern but reconstructed zero kill-chain phases.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Alert on node/npm processes making outbound HTTP/HTTPS or DNS requests to non-whitelisted domains during or immediately after package installation. Flag unexpected C2 domains or high-entropy subdomains from npm process context.

suggestion.title: Credential Enumeration via Environment Variables During npm Install
suggestion.rationale: Article states malware targets 'npm, GitHub, AWS, and Kubernetes Secrets' — implies environment variable enumeration. Pipeline failed to map T1552.003 or generate detection for getenv/process.env enumeration.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Detect node processes spawning 'env', 'printenv', or equivalent commands during npm install, or repeated /proc/self/environ reads. Flag code patterns accessing process.env in postinstall context.

suggestion.title: Suspicious npm Registry Substitution or Malicious Package Version Pinning
suggestion.rationale: Article describes Shai-Hulud as supply-chain attack via npm — pipeline should reconstruct T1195.001 (Supply Chain Compromise) and detect when package.json/package-lock.json references unexpected registry URLs or pinned malicious versions.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Monitor .npmrc, package.json, package-lock.json for registry substitution (pointing to non-official npm). Alert on installation of packages with unexpected or typosquatted names, or version pinning to specific malicious hashes known in threat intel feeds.

suggestion.title: Multi-credential Harvest and Batch Exfiltration
suggestion.rationale: Article headline specifies *multiple* credential types (npm + GitHub + AWS + Kubernetes) stolen in single attack — pipeline should have generated composite detection combining credential access (T1552) with exfiltration (T1567) but returned zero use cases.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Alert when single process accesses multiple credential stores sequentially (e.g., reads ~/.ssh, then ~/.aws, then ~/.kube within <5 min window) followed by outbound traffic to non-standard ports or to domains outside developer infrastructure.

suggestion.title: npm Package Installation Spawns Unauthorized Credential File Access
suggestion.rationale: Article establishes the attack occurs during package installation ('worm steals npm, GitHub, AWS, Kubernetes secrets from developers'). Detection must correlate npm/node process execution with reads of .npmrc, .aws/credentials, .kube/config, .git/config files.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Node.js or npm processes reading sensitive credential files (.npmrc, .aws/credentials, .kube/config, .git/config) shortly after package installation. Establish baseline of legitimate npm credential access and alert on anomalous reads from unfamiliar package paths.

suggestion.title: Multi-Secret Type Exfiltration from Single Developer Host
suggestion.rationale: Article title explicitly names four credential types stolen: npm, GitHub, AWS, Kubernetes. This pattern is highly specific to supply-chain malware targeting DevOps/platform engineers.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Single process or child process reading multiple secret file types followed by outbound network connections to non-standard ports or C2 domains. Correlate file access telemetry (reading .npmrc, AWS SDK config dirs, .kube/config) with subsequent DNS/HTTP egress from same process parent.

suggestion.title: Malicious npm Package Detection via Suspicious Post-Install Script Behavior
suggestion.rationale: Worm propagates via npm packages themselves. Article describes active credential theft during installation, suggesting malicious postinstall hooks or binary downloads.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: npm install or package manager spawning unexpected child processes (shell, curl, powershell) that access credential files or make outbound connections. Flag postinstall scripts executing network-enabled binaries or credential enumeration commands.

suggestion.title: npm Supply-Chain Compromise Indicator: Registry Tampering or Typosquatting Package Detection
suggestion.rationale: Article title indicates the worm distributes via npm packages. Requires identifying malicious package names, versions, or registry metadata anomalies.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Monitor npm package.json lock files for unexpected package versions, newly introduced dependencies not in prior snapshots, or packages with recent creation dates and high download velocity. Cross-reference against threat-intel feeds of known-malicious npm packages.

suggestion.title: Kubernetes kubeconfig Credential Extraction and Lateral Movement Preparation
suggestion.rationale: Article names Kubernetes secrets as exfiltration target. kubeconfig access enables cluster-wide lateral movement and should be isolated as high-fidelity indicator.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: File read access to $HOME/.kube/config or KUBECONFIG environment variable by non-kubectl processes. Correlate with subsequent process execution of kubectl or cloud CLI tools that would use extracted tokens.

suggestion.title: AWS Credential Harvesting from ~/.aws/credentials and Environment Variables
suggestion.rationale: Article explicitly names AWS secret theft. $HOME/.aws/credentials and AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID environment variable reads are high-confidence indicators of lateral-movement preparation in cloud environments.
suggestion.detection_hypothesis: Process reading $HOME/.aws/credentials, $HOME/.aws/config, or accessing AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY environment variables outside of AWS CLI/SDK context. Establish whitelist of expected AWS credential consumers and alert on anomalies.
